title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a harbour clean-up project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Marta, The Supply Coordinator
Marta joined the harbour clean-up because she walked along the pier every evening and was tired of seeing plastic trapped between the rocks. She was soon asked to coordinate gloves, bags and litter pickers. [47]Her first challenge was getting the storage shed opened on time, because the harbour office changed shifts just before the volunteers arrived on Saturday mornings.[/47] When the key was late, everyone lost energy standing around. [48]Her experience running a small café helped her keep stock lists, replacement orders and refreshment costs under control.[/48] She also learned which biscuits disappeared fastest after two hours in the wind. Marta says the clean-up taught her that morale is practical: dry socks and working equipment matter as much as speeches about the environment. By the end of the season, she could tell from the tide and the weather how many bags the group would fill. The rhythm now feels familiar.
texts.B:
### Felix, The School Group Leader
[49]Felix became involved after a science teacher who knew him from kayaking lessons asked him to guide school groups safely around the harbour wall.[/49] He liked the water but had never managed thirty excited pupils holding litter pickers. [50]He was surprised by the amount of permission paperwork required before children could collect rubbish near slippery stones and moored boats.[/50] The forms seemed excessive until one pupil nearly stepped backwards into a coil of rope. Felix developed simple rules: stay in pairs, point before picking, and never reach under anything sharp. His best moment came when a boy who had complained all morning found a plastic ring tangled around seaweed and suddenly became serious. Felix says the work changed how he teaches. Instead of giving long warnings, he now creates short routines that help young people behave safely without feeling controlled. He also learned that excitement can become responsibility very quickly.
texts.C:
### Naomi, The Tide Board Painter
Naomi, a sign painter, volunteered after repeatedly waiting for the ferry beside a cracked information board that nobody seemed to read. [51]During those waits, she noticed visitors dropping bottles near the bins because they could not tell which container was for recycling.[/51] She offered to repaint the board and add clearer instructions. [55]She used large symbols and simple colour blocks so people could understand the clean-up zones and waste categories from a distance.[/55] [52]The most discouraging day was when a storm tore down her first set of laminated signs and scattered them along the beach.[/52] Naomi felt guilty about creating more rubbish, but the team collected every sheet and helped her attach the replacements properly. She says public information must be built for salt, rain and impatient people. A beautiful sign is useless if it cannot survive the first rough weekend. She now carries extra cord in her bag. now.
texts.D:
### Idris, The Bird Rescue Contact
[53]Idris volunteered because his aunt, a birdwatcher with limited mobility, loved the harbour but was distressed by seeing gulls caught in fishing line.[/53] He did not handle injured birds himself, but he became the link between cleaners and the local wildlife rescue group. [54]His hardest task was organising reports and cordoned-off areas without blocking the narrow path used by walkers, wheelchairs and delivery trolleys.[/54] He learned to speak calmly when people wanted to help but were standing too close. Idris was relieved the first time a tangled gull was collected safely. [56]He is now creating a laminated contact guide so future volunteers know exactly who to call instead of relying on his phone.[/56] He says the harbour is shared by commuters, tourists and animals, so care has to be organised rather than improvised. That idea keeps him returning. The role is quieter than rescue, but just as necessary. every season.

questions.question: mentions a shift-change problem that delayed access to essential supplies?
questions.explanation: Marta says the harbour office changed shifts just before volunteers arrived, delaying the shed key.

questions.question: used experience from running a business to manage supplies and costs?
questions.explanation: Marta’s café experience helped her keep stock lists, orders and refreshment costs under control.

questions.question: was invited because someone knew they had relevant outdoor skills?
questions.explanation: Felix was asked by a science teacher who knew him from kayaking lessons.

questions.question: was surprised by the paperwork needed before children could work in a potentially risky place?
questions.explanation: Felix was surprised by permission paperwork for pupils collecting rubbish near stones and boats.

questions.question: noticed a problem with public information while waiting for transport?
questions.explanation: Naomi noticed people dropping bottles near bins because the recycling container was unclear.

questions.question: describes a discouraging setback caused by bad weather?
questions.explanation: A storm tore down Naomi’s first laminated signs.

questions.question: joined because a relative was upset by harm to wildlife in the area?
questions.explanation: Idris volunteered because his aunt was distressed by gulls caught in fishing line.

questions.question: had to organise protective measures without blocking other users of the path?
questions.explanation: Idris organised reports and cordoned-off areas without blocking the narrow path.

questions.question: used visual design to make zones and categories clear from a distance?
questions.explanation: Naomi used symbols and colour blocks for clean-up zones and waste categories.

questions.question: is creating a contact guide so future volunteers know what to do without relying on them?
questions.explanation: Idris is creating a laminated contact guide.
